conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have a child - my son Alex. He's 18 and still living at home with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few questions.

Since Alex is 18, an important factor is whether he's in approved education or training. Is Alex currently enrolled in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is doing his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. A-levels would typically count as non-advanced education.

Could you tell me when Alex turned 18, or what date his 18th birthday was?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I should mention - Alex is already 18, he turned 18 a little while ago. He's currently in his A-level studies now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. To properly assess eligibility, I need to know more specifically when Alex turned 18. 

Could you tell me the actual date (or at least the month and year) when Alex turned 18?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not entirely sure of the exact date off the top of my head - I'd have to check his birth certificate for that. But what I can tell you is that he's 18 now and he's still doing his A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's okay, I understand. Let me ask it a different way that might be easier:

Has Alex already started his final year of A-levels, or is he still in an earlier year of his A-level course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's currently in his A-level course. I'm not sure which exact year, but he's definitely still studying and hasn't finished yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask one more important question to help determine eligibility:

Is Alex enrolled in A-levels on a full-time basis (not part-time)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's doing his A-levels full-time at the sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

Are you the person responsible for Alex - meaning, does he live with you and are you responsible for his upbringing and care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives here at home with me, and I'm responsible for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. One final important question:

Has Alex left full-time education at any point since turning 16, or has he continued in education continuously?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's continued in education - he's been studying continuously since he was 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also check - are you currently living in the UK, and is Alex living in the UK as well?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK - here in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News!

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, Alex meets all the criteria for Child Benefit as a young person aged 16-19 in approved education:

✓ Alex is 18 years old (under 20)
✓ He's enrolled in full-time A-level studies (non-advanced education)
✓ He's been in continuous education since age 16
✓ He lives with you and you're responsible for him
✓ You both live in the UK

**Important to note:** Child Benefit for young people in education continues until the earlier of:
- The date they turn 20, **or**
- The 31st August following their 19th birthday, **or**
- When they leave full-time education

So as long as Alex continues his full-time A-level studies, you can claim Child Benefit for him until one of those dates is reached.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit online or by post. If you have any other questions, feel free to ask!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and enrolled in full-time A-level studies at sixth form college. A-levels are considered non-advanced education (below degree level). Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for a young person aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Alex meets this criterion as he is: (1) under 20 years old, (2) enrolled in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), (3) has continued in education continuously since age 16, and (4) lives with the claimant who is responsible for him. Both the claimant and Alex live in the UK. Child Benefit can continue until Alex turns 20, or until the earlier of: when he leaves education, or the 31st August following his 19th birthday (whichever comes first). Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college. Alex has been in continuous education since age 16 and lives at home with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex as he meets the criteria for young people aged 16-19 in approved full-time education.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and enrolled in full-time A-level studies at sixth form college. A-levels are considered non-advanced education (below degree level). Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for a young person aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Alex meets this criterion as he is: (1) under 20 years old, (2) enrolled in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), (3) has continued in education continuously since age 16, and (4) lives with the claimant who is responsible for him. Both the claimant and Alex live in the UK. Child Benefit can continue until Alex turns 20, or until the earlier of: when he leaves education, or the 31st August following his 19th birthday (whichever comes first). Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college. Alex has been in continuous education since age 16 and lives at home with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex as he meets the criteria for young people aged 16-19 in approved full-time education.
